title: From Data to Decisions: How SAP is Reimagining Enterprise Intelligence for the AI Era
description: As enterprises race to adopt AI, SAP believes the real competitive advantage will not come from more data but from better context. As enterprises accelerate their AI journeys, the conversation around data is undergoing a fundamental shift. On the sidelines of the SAP Sapphire Event 2026 in Orlando last week, Shipra Sinha, senior analyst, Industry Intelligence Group (IIG), CyberMedia Research (CMR), spoke with Dan Yu, chief marketing officer, SAP Business Data Cloud. In this interaction, Dan Yu discusses why the future of enterprise intelligence will depend less on data volume and more on business context, trust, governance, and semantic understanding. From Business Data Cloud and knowledge graphs to AI agents and open ecosystems, the discussion explores how SAP is reimagining modernisation, enterprise decision-making, and the evolving relationship between humans and AI in the next era of digital transformation. For years, enterprise data strategies were centred on scale, with bigger databases, faster analytics, and smarter dashboards driving digital transformation. Today, AI is reshaping that conversation. Decision-making is no longer confined to IT teams or CIO offices but is becoming a boardroom priority across business functions. At the centre of this shift, SAP is positioning itself beyond software through initiatives like Business Data Cloud, knowledge graphs, and AI agents, with a focus on helping enterprises move from simply storing data to understanding the business context behind it. As Dan Yu explained during the conversation, “It’s not about as much data as possible anymore. It’s about the right context of data.” Moving Beyond Traditional Data Thinking One of the biggest challenges enterprises face today is that many organisations still think about data in traditional terms. Rows, columns, dashboards, and reports continue to dominate enterprise systems. But AI requires more than disconnected information. It needs meaning, relationships, governance, and an understanding of how businesses actually operate. SAP’s approach centres on building what it calls a 'business knowledge layer'. Through its knowledge graph and data products, the company aims to connect data across departments, workflows, and applications while attaching business meaning and operational context to it. In large organisations, critical knowledge often exists in silos. Finance systems operate separately from supply chains. Planning teams rely on disconnected environments. Important business logic often sits within applications or with employees themselves. SAP believes contextualising this information is essential for both humans and AI systems to make better decisions. The company’s Business Data Cloud strategy is designed around this idea. Instead of forcing enterprises to move all their data into a single ecosystem, SAP is building an open data fabric architecture that allows organisations to work across platforms like Databricks, Snowflake, hyperscalers, and on-premise environments. The objective is not simply integration. It is creating a trusted business layer that can unify and govern distributed enterprise data. Redefining Modernization SAP also believes enterprises need to rethink what modernisation actually means. For years, modernisation was largely associated with migration. Moving workloads to the cloud or replacing legacy systems became the benchmark for progress. But many organisations are now realising that migration alone rarely delivers meaningful business value. According to SAP, true modernisation is about improving business processes, increasing productivity, and enabling organisations to operate at greater scale. This thinking is also reshaping the way software is developed internally. Traditional enterprise software cycles that once involved years of planning and development are giving way to faster, customer-driven innovation models, particularly around AI. The company’s growing portfolio of AI-powered capabilities now spans planning, analytics, workflow management, financial operations, and enterprise productivity. SAP increasingly describes this shift as moving from “human scale to AI scale", where intelligent systems support employees, simplify complex workflows, and improve decision-making. Why Trust Matters More Than Data Movement While enterprises continue to generate massive volumes of information across cloud platforms and ecosystems, SAP argues that data movement itself is no longer the primary challenge. The bigger issue is trust. Modern architectures already allow enterprises to process information across distributed environments. What businesses now need is confidence in the quality, governance, and meaning of the information being used by AI systems. Without trusted definitions, semantic understanding, and governance frameworks, even advanced AI systems can produce unreliable outcomes. This is one reason SAP is emphasising openness and interoperability across its ecosystem partnerships. Collaborations with companies such as Databricks, Snowflake, AWS, and others reflect a broader recognition that enterprise AI cannot operate in isolated environments. At the same time, openness must coexist with governance, especially in industries handling sensitive or regulated data. As Dan Yu noted, 'Open' does not mean 'irresponsible'. You can be open and still governed, secure, and compliant at the same time.” That balance between accessibility and control is likely to become one of the defining challenges of enterprise AI adoption. The Rise of Context Aware AI AI is also transforming how enterprises interact with software itself. Traditional business intelligence systems relied heavily on dashboards and visualisation tools where employees manually interpreted charts, tables, and reports. AI is now making those interactions more conversational and contextual. Instead of relying only on predefined reports, enterprises are beginning to adopt AI systems capable of understanding broader business intent. Users can ask more dynamic questions around operational risks, planning assumptions, bottlenecks, or business performance. For that to work effectively, AI systems need more than raw data. They need structured business understanding. This is why semantic layers, governance frameworks, knowledge graphs, and contextual definitions are becoming foundational to enterprise AI strategies. The shift is not simply about faster analytics. It is about making enterprise systems more aware of how businesses function in real time. The Human Side of AI Transformation Despite the rapid pace of AI innovation, SAP believes the bigger challenge may still be cultural. Across industries, organisations continue to grapple with concerns around job displacement, operational disruption, and workforce readiness. Enterprises that frame AI purely as a replacement tool may struggle to build trust internally. Instead, SAP sees AI as an enabler that can help employees focus on higher-value work while reducing repetitive and manual tasks. This also means AI readiness can no longer remain the responsibility of IT teams alone. Organisations will need stronger governance cultures, broader AI literacy, and closer alignment between business and technology leadership. The transformation underway is no longer just digital. It is organisational, operational, and human. And as enterprises prepare for the next phase of AI adoption, SAP believes one idea is becoming increasingly clear: in the future of enterprise intelligence, context will matter more than volume. By Shipra Sinha, Senior Analyst, CyberMedia Research (CMR)
